chapters.title: Traditions Are Choices, Not Inevitabilities
chapters.text:
Traditions feel timeless, yet human choices build them. Many Americans assume Dutch settlers imported Santa Claus, but Protestant migrants did not center St. Nicholas on Christmas the way Dutch Catholics had on his feast day. New Yorkers later remade the figure and tied it to December 25.

A group of New Yorkers reinvented the Dutch tradition and made Santa a symbol of Christmas in the United States.

Rituals therefore track decisions: daring to print a calendar with Christmas marked; founding the New-York Historical Society to recover an imagined past of class harmony; printing Santa's image to sell newspapers. Under the right conditions, even a single household's habit can nationalize.

Economics mattered as well. Agrarian and industrial America judged the season differently along a scale from acceptable to awkward.

It is useful to think of traditions not as static entities but as dynamic forces that are constantly being negotiated and renegotiated. ~ Stephen Nissenbaum, PhD

Stephen Nissenbaum,

This history follows a season Americans nearly abandoned — then rebuilt into the holiday we recognize.

chapters.title: Puritan Resistance and a Rowdier Holiday
chapters.text:
Agrarian December followed harvest and winter prep — a natural pause for feasting once beer and wine had finished fermenting. Echoes remain in "The Twelve Days of Christmas" and Twelfth Night.

Alcohol often resulted in aggression or disorders, and at that time, people closely associated this excessive behavior with the Christmas season.

New England Puritans attacked excess rather than the calendar date itself. Seventeenth-century almanacs omitted December 25. From 1659, celebrating could cost five shillings — evidence that observance continued, though prosecutions appear rare.

Even opponents admitted the season was special. Behavioral limits widened into carnival: cross-dressing, masking, apprentices mocking masters, children ridiculing gentlemen. The period became a venue for challenging hierarchy and pressing claims of justice.

Did you know? The puritans even changed the names of weekdays to purify the almanacs. For example, they knew well that the fourth day indicated Thor, the god of thunder in Norse mythology.

chapters.title: Carols, Misrule, and Shifting Compromise
chapters.text:
Carnival logic inverted authority. Schoolboys barricaded classrooms until masters granted holidays — parents tacitly approved even though teachers lost paid days. Wassailers sang at wealthy doors, blending blessings with thinly veiled threats when hosts refused food or coin. Polite verses in modern carols descend from that bargaining.

Elites opened houses to the poor and gave seasonal gifts to dependents whose goodwill mattered agriculturally. The practice was not purely generous: broken windows, yearly exploitation, and slaveholders withholding presents to enforce obedience all belonged to the same system.

Seventeenth-century elites treated misrule as a pressure valve that preserved patriarchy. After Puritan pressure eased, restoration governments restored the holiday to calendars; John Tully's almanac publicized saints' days again. Power shifted, bans returned, then softened.

By 1712 a prominent minister proposed compromise: celebrate December 25 without drunken disorder. Almanac writers spread the idea. Editors still defended including Christmas in 1758; by 1760 the date appeared routinely in red ink.

Mentioning Christmas in the calendar until 1730 was dangerous. Yet since 1760, all have marked the 25th of December in red ink.

Almanacs alone did not reinvent the season. Santa Claus would do much of that work.

chapters.title: Moore, Pintard, and the Reinvention of Santa
chapters.text:
Santa's American story intertwines with John Pintard, who remembered childhood Christmases as moments of cross-class harmony. Early nineteenth-century New York looked harsher: industrial wealth no longer depended on peasant goodwill, and misrule turned vindictive — noisy crowds harassing wealthy neighborhoods until elites often avoided celebrating publicly.

Pintard responded by founding the New-York Historical Society in 1804 and elevating St. Nicholas as civic patron, funding broadsides that spread the saint's image. Washington Irving's 1819 Christmas sketches romanticized old-fashioned warmth and, alongside later Dickensian fiction, supplied the sentimental postcard version of the holiday — though translating image into practice took decades.

Clement Clarke Moore's 1823 poem "A Visit from St. Nicholas" supplied the breakthrough. An 1821 anonymous tale had already paired St. Nicholas with a sleigh and one reindeer; Moore added seven more and recast Santa as a compact, fur-clad figure whose short pipe signaled working-class roots rather than episcopal grandeur. He brought gifts, never punishment.

"A Visit from St. Nicholas" wasn't just a fairy tale for kids. It rethought the role of the poor: they did not come to request but to give.

chapters.title: Merchants, Mammoth Cakes, and the Domestic Holiday
chapters.text:
Moore's poem helped normalize home-centered Christmas, even while bars still poured free drinks and wassailing filled streets. Newspapers amplified domestic virtue and framed public disorder as criminal, making private celebration fashionable.

Commerce followed. Gift buying required safe streets and eager shoppers, so merchants and editors promoted indoor rituals. December advertisements surged; by the 1830s journalists warned readers to budget against manipulative sales tactics.

Ads first appeared in newspapers along with the story about St. Nicholas. The target audience was primarily children; since 1809, gifts for women have also gained popularity. After 1820, the number of commercials surged; editors published supplements to post them. Soon the situation started to resemble a modern one. In 1834 journalists wrote that people had to meticulously plan their purchases to avoid falling into the traps of merchants that prompted customers to buy unnecessary things.

Shops erected life-size Santas; confectioners baked "mammoth cakes" approaching a thousand pounds to lure families. Books dominated early gift ads — illustrated gift volumes with inscription pages became bestsellers segmented by politics, age, and class. Personal Bibles, including children's editions, rounded out the market.

chapters.title: How Literature Spread the Christmas Tree
chapters.text:
The decorated tree completed the familiar holiday picture. Popular legends tied its arrival to Hessian troops or Queen Victoria's German husband, yet dates rarely match records. Even German Pennsylvanians seldom used trees before the 1810s.

Although the Christmas tree is a German custom, the truth is more complicated. From the 17th century to 1750, this tradition was widespread only in Strasbourg. The turning point was in 1771 when a special guest, Johann Wolfgang von Goethe, visited this city. He popularized Strasbourg's custom in his books. Yet the elite in Berlin accepted it only in the 1810s. After 20 years, this ritual finally transformed into a national practice.

Because of literature, Christmas trees became widespread in the United States and Germany simultaneously.

Information about the Christmas tree was diffused by means of commercial literature, not via immigrant folk culture—from the top down, not from the bottom up. ~ Stephen Nissenbaum, PhD

Stephen Nissenbaum,

Samuel Taylor Coleridge's 1824 American publication described German children offering handmade gifts to parents — a detail that reassured U.S. readers worried about spoiled youth. Catharine Sedgwick's "New Year's Day" (1835) placed a tree inside an American home; publishers folded the story into gift books sold nationwide.

Did you know? Catharine Sedgwick must have learned about the Christmas tree from Charles Folien, a Harvard professor and a German migrant. He was an active abolitionist and aimed to liberate the enslaved people. Charles Folien adhered to his principles even when many friends alienated him.

chapters.title: Dickens, Charity Dinners, and Class Tension
chapters.text:
Wealthy Americans still debated how to relate to the poor at Christmas — direct gifts, charity, or distance. Dickens's "A Christmas Carol" offered a template: Scrooge donates, sends a turkey to the Cratchits, and visits family, treating each class with tailored generosity.

Press campaigns, charities, and fiction urged similar gestures. Stories claimed that feeding children would narrow class divides; sponsored Christmas dinners for poor youth became routine, sometimes with donors watching thousands eat.

Theodore Roosevelt's financial support helped arrange Christmas dinners from 1870 to 1873.

Shared hymns and press coverage portrayed these meals as moments when class boundaries vanished — at least symbolically. Yet the ritual cut both ways. Elite children were harder to surprise each year, while women orchestrating home celebrations faced exhaustion and post-holiday illness.

Some poor attendees resisted spectacle. Newspaper boys hurled utensils and pies — not because they rejected food, but because they resented being displayed.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Christmas mirrors the society celebrating it. Early observance blended seasonal release with disorder — inversion of hierarchy, bargaining carols, and gifts exchanged under social pressure.

Industrialization and marketing retired much of that carnivalesque chaos. Writers, merchants, and civic leaders reframed December 25 as a domestic, child-centered holiday with trees, Santa letters, and store-bought presents.

Literature accelerated the shift, yet it did not invent every custom from nothing. Often authors described practices readers were ready to adopt — children exchanging handmade gifts moved Americans as much as any editorial.

The deeper lesson: traditions stay alive because people renew them. Families can still author meaning deliberately.

Try this:


• Create an inspirational playlist with tracks that bring good memories and a festive mood. It could be songs about Christmas or others you listen to during the holidays.
• Ask your relatives about how they celebrated this special night in childhood. What is their favorite tradition? Is there something they want to return?
• Think about a custom that can make this Christmas unforgettable. It could be a surprise or unusual activity.

Read it till the end?
